narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 37, middle-income family background, part-time enrollment status, and school deciles of 4th in Year 1 and 7th in Year 3—and calculated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your planned part-time status, your Year 3 grade decile, and your Year 1 grade decile. Most influential was your choice to study part-time, which is uncommon (only about 7% of students do this) and strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, likely because part-time students in the data often faced greater challenges balancing commitments. On a positive note, your 7th-place decile in Year 3 grades—better than the typical student's 5.5th-place average—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward bar passage success. However, your 4th-place decile in Year 1 grades, below the average of about 5.7th place, pulled in the opposite direction, raising concerns about early academic momentum. Your LSAT score of 37, slightly above average, and solid undergraduate GPA helped balance things somewhat, but couldn't overcome the top concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have stronger early grades and full-time plans, your profile signaled higher risk despite some strengths like your later-year improvement. To move forward, consider reapplying after boosting your early-grade equivalent through additional coursework or test prep, switching to a full-time track if feasible, and gathering evidence like internships or references showing your bar prep discipline. You might also target schools with more flexible policies for part-time or diverse backgrounds like yours. With targeted improvements, your upward grade trend positions you well for a stronger prediction next time. Keep pushing—many students turn these setbacks into bar-passing success stories.
